retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Osteoradionecrosis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Calcifying crown Odontoma Periapical abscess Periapical granuloma Rarefying and condensing osteitis

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Evaluation -- Central Giant Cell Granuloma
retrieved_docs.content: Radiographic assessment is not pathognomonic and may vary. It will show a radiolucent lesion, which may be small around the root of a tooth or large, involving most of the jaw, resorbing the cortical plate and adjacent teeth. For small lesions, plain radiographs may suffice. For large, aggressively destructive lesions, 3-dimensional CT imaging will provide a more complete picture of the bony destruction. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- History and Physical -- Central Giant Cell Granuloma
retrieved_docs.content: Central giant cell granuloma may be indolent, causing a painless bony swelling. [24] When aggressive, it causes a rapidly enlarging painful swelling, displacing teeth, resorbing roots, and the cortical plate of the alveolar ridge. Intra-orally, a blue, brown discoloration may be seen due to thinning of the cortical plate and overlying mucosal. Clinical expansion occurs over a period of a fortnight to a couple of months. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Evaluation -- Central Giant Cell Granuloma
retrieved_docs.content: When assessing central giant cell granulomas, it is important to exclude any underlying systemic diagnosis. Hyperparathyroidism will lead to brown tumors, which will occur as multiple osteoclastic giant cell tumors. Cherubism will cause bilateral lesions in the mandible of young children. Bloods work assessing bone profile and PTH levels is important, and family history might highlight potential cherubism. [10] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Central Giant Cell Granuloma
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to distinguish central giant cell granuloma from its systemic counterparts. Clinicians, therefore, must have a broad appreciation of what multiple or bilateral lesions may imply and must carry out a relevant biochemical investigation and familial screening.

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Differential Diagnosis -- Central giant cell granuloma
retrieved_docs.content: Giant cell granulomas can be seen in other systemic conditions; it is, therefore, vital to exclude these. Brown’s tumor of hyperparathyroidism – histologically indistinguishable but normally present in multiple and with deranged bone profile results. Cherubism – this is an autosomal dominant disorder, most commonly caused by a mutation in SH3P2. It causes widespread and multiple osteolytic lesions of, primarily, the posterior mandible. Onset is in childhood, producing a classical, symmetric full-cheek appearance. [27] Noonan syndrome Neurofibromatosis type I Aneurysmal bone cysts

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Introduction -- Central Giant Cell Granuloma
retrieved_docs.content: Central giant cell granuloma is the most common non-odontogenic tumor that affects the jaws, accounting for 7% of benign jaw tumors. [9] [10] They are most commonly found in the anterior portion of the mandible but can affect the maxilla. They are benign but can be locally destructive. [11] They are of osteoclastic origin and present, therefore, as expansile lytic lesions. They are usually unifocal; in the presence of multifocal or bilateral lesions underlying diagnoses of hyperparathyroidism or cherubism should be investigated.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Evaluation -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Dental radiographs can show the extent of the infection and help evaluate areas that are not obvious to visual evaluation. These include intraoral periapical radiographs, bitewing radiographs, and panoramic radiographs. These radiographs help to identify the extent of dental caries, the presence of a periapical abscess, alveolar bone loss concerning periodontitis, and third molar eruptions. The dental practitioner’s physical examination must confirm all radiographic evidence for a full evaluation and accurate diagnosis. CT scans and MRIs help assess deep-space infections and osteomyelitis. Cone-beam CT is becoming increasingly common in clinical settings to better visualize areas of oral infections. [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Evaluation -- Alveolar Osteitis
retrieved_docs.content: Alveolar osteitis is a clinical diagnosis and does not require laboratory studies or radiographic examination. A thorough history and physical exam are sufficient to diagnose this postoperative complication. However, panoramic radiographs can be performed if there is a concern for a retained tooth fragment within the alveolar socket. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Necrotizing Periodontal Diseases -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Radiographic findings range from minimal to extensive changes. Osteonecrosis may appear as a moth-eaten, patchy radiolucency, similar to features seen in medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw, osteoradionecrosis, or osteomyelitis. Radiopaque sequestra may also be present. Computed tomography offers greater detail and aids in distinguishing among osseous pathologies. A definitive diagnosis depends on clinical findings and patient history.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Osteoradionecrosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The evaluation should include: Review radiotherapy and dental record Biopsy of wound Panographic Radiographs CT of mandible Stage the disease Rule out recurrence/persistence of primary malignancy or new secondary malignancy by getting a biopsy of the wound. [9] [10] [11] [8] Imaging studies aid in the diagnosis and quantify the extent of disease. CT findings: Cortical bone disruption with mixed lysis and sclerosis can be seen. Pathologic fractures can also be seen. Panographic radiograph findings: Plain radiographs are useful for screening but can underestimate the extent of lesions. Lytic areas with ill-defined, non-sclerotic borders can be seen. Gather record of radiotherapy and review treatment method, radiation portal, and total dose. Marx Staging of ORN is the most common staging system in use. It is based on response to hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBO).

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Histopathology -- Central Giant Cell Granuloma
retrieved_docs.content: Histology shows these lesions to be localized, lobulated, and have osteolytic proliferations. Vascular connective tissue is observed, with a patchy distribution of multinucleated giant cells in the surrounding stoma. Extravasated erythrocytes and hemosiderin deposits are frequently seen, highlighting multifocal hemorrhage. [25] [26] [27] Occasionally trabecular woven bone is observed. [10] These giant cells carry osteoclastic receptors, bind to osteoclast-specific monoclonal antibodies, and respond to calcitonin. They are therefore considered to be precursors to or represent osteoclasts. [38] More aggressive versions have been shown to have more numerous, larger giant cells with a greater surface area density. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Cheilitis Granulomatosa -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Immunologic factors: CG is a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by a predominant T helper 1-mediated immune response. The main anomaly is most likely a local alteration of innate immunity of lip mucosa in response to various antigens whose insufficient purification leads to a persistent granulomatous reaction.

retrieved_docs.title: Actinomycosis -- History and Physical -- Cervicofacial
retrieved_docs.content: This is the most common, constituting approximately 60% of infections and usually involves the tissues surrounding the maxilla and lower mandible (almost 50% of all cases). The most common presentation is a progressive, painless mass that is indurated with or without reddish or bluish discoloration of overlying skin and eventually evolves into multiple abscesses with sinus tract formation, which drains yellow sulfur granules. The patient may give a history of an oral procedure or injury. Difficulty in chewing food and trismus can occur in chronic stages of infection. Lymphadenopathy is not associated with the disease process.

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Complications -- Central Giant Cell Granuloma
retrieved_docs.content: Complications can occur due to the destructive nature of central giant cell lesions, particularly loss of dentition or tooth buds.  When cortical bone erosion occurs around nerves, specifically the inferior alveolar nerve, impingement can occur. As it does not exhibit perineural invasion but only impingement, once repositioned, the inferior alveolar nerve function is restored. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Differential Diagnosis -- Examples of These Lesions
retrieved_docs.content: Dentigerous cyst: It usually presents as a unilocular translucent lesion that causes painless bony expansion around the impacted third molar tooth. [65] Traumatic bone cyst: It usually presents as a unilocular translucent lesion affecting the mandibular molar and premolar region in young adults. It always spares the inferior alveolar canal. [66] Aneurysmal bone cyst: It mainly affects the long bones and vertebral column of young adults rather than jaws. It can either present as unilocular or multilocular translucent lesions with marked cortical expansion and teeth displacement. [67]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ and ربط البوق are medical concepts that frequently co‑occur, indicating that the presence of حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ is often associated with the finding or procedure ربط البوق. This consistent association suggests that clinicians should be aware of the link between these two entities, as recognizing one may prompt evaluation for the other, thereby informing diagnosis and guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is clinically associated with nonspecific chest pain and with fatigue (التَّعَب). These findings indicate that patients with oral candidiasis may present not only with typical oral lesions but also with systemic or atypical symptoms such as unexplained chest discomfort and generalized tiredness, underscoring the importance of considering this fungal infection in the differential diagnosis when such nonspecific complaints arise.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2806
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion is identified as a diagnostic finding associated with the disease سرطانة العظم, indicating that secondary tumor deposits have occurred within bone tissue. The presence of a metastatic lesion in a patient suspected of having سرطانة العظم suggests disease spread beyond the primary site, which has important implications for staging, prognosis, and therapeutic planning. Recognizing metastatic lesions through imaging or pathology therefore aids clinicians in assessing the extent of سرطانة العظم, guiding decisions about systemic therapy, surgical intervention, and overall patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of the disease سرطانة العظم. In patients with سرطانة العظم, the identification of metastatic lesions indicates that malignant cells have spread to bone from another primary site, while secondary lesions represent additional tumor deposits that develop as a consequence of the primary bone malignancy. Recognizing these lesions is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and the formulation of appropriate treatment strategies for سرطانة العظم.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” denotes that radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) is recognized as an acceptable therapeutic option for patients with tongue disorders (اِضطِراب اللسان), indicating that the use of radiotherapy is permitted and appropriate in the management of this specific oral condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is identified as a pathological finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating the presence of infiltrating malignant cells within the affected bone tissue. Likewise, Tumor cells, malignant are recognized as the abnormal cellular component that characterizes سرطانة العظم, reflecting the disease’s neoplastic nature. Together, these findings underscore that both the infiltrative pattern of malignant cells and the presence of malignant tumor cells are key diagnostic features of سرطانة العظم, aiding clinicians in confirming the diagnosis and guiding appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is mapped to ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي, indicating that this malignant condition involves the upper jaw and reflects a neoplastic process affecting both bone and connective tissue structures in that region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2232
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary Lesion is recognized as a diagnostic finding associated with سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). In the clinical evaluation of patients with سرطانة العظم, the identification of secondary lesions indicates disease spread or metastatic involvement, which is essential for accurate staging, prognosis assessment, and formulation of appropriate therapeutic strategies. Recognizing secondary lesions therefore plays a critical role in the comprehensive management of bone cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2655
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of سرطانة العظم, indicating the presence of bone cancer. The identification of a malignant cellular infiltrate reflects the invasion of cancerous cells into bone tissue, while a secondary lesion denotes additional disease sites that arise as the primary bone tumor progresses. Recognizing these findings together aids clinicians in confirming the diagnosis of سرطانة العظم, assessing disease extent, and guiding appropriate therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2768
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain, a symptom that lacks a clearly defined cause, has been reported to be clinically associated with oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي). This relationship suggests that patients who present with unexplained chest discomfort may warrant evaluation for an underlying fungal infection of the oral cavity, particularly when other risk factors for candidiasis are present. Recognizing this association can help clinicians adopt a more comprehensive diagnostic approach and ensure appropriate treatment for both the chest pain and any concurrent oral candidiasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1540
retrieved_docs.content: Intrakavitární radioterapie is a specialized form of radiation therapy that targets lesions within body cavities; the two terms are closely related and may be considered synonymous in clinical practice, reflecting the same therapeutic approach of delivering ionizing radiation to treat intracavitary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2168
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary Lesion is identified as a finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating that the presence of secondary lesions may support the diagnosis of this bone malignancy. Conversely, Tumor cells, benign are not considered a finding of سرطانة العظم, meaning that benign tumor cells do not contribute to the identification or confirmation of this disease.
